curated.title: Lot Line — address-radius watch for development next door
curated.one_liner: Type your home address once and get a plain-language alert the moment a rezoning, variance, or major-development case is filed within the legal notice radius of your parcel — with a one-tap path to the actual hearing and comment deadline.
curated.problem: Homeowners and renters find out a 5-story building or variance next door is happening only when bulldozers arrive, or when a jargon-filled paper notice card gets tossed with junk mail. The statutory mailed notice (300/500 ft) is the only systematic signal residents get, and it has no map, no deadline reminder, and no action path. People who care about their block have no proactive way to learn 'something is being proposed near me.'
curated.target_user: Homeowners, renters, and small landlords in mid-to-large US cities who care about their immediate block but are not planning nerds — the person who would have shown up to the hearing if they'd known in time and understood the stakes.
curated.solution: MapIt-style address->parcel geocoding establishes the user's parcel centroid. A scheduled job polls city zoning-case ArcGIS REST feature services and BLDS permit feeds on Socrata, normalizes each case to an OCD Event, and runs a spatial query: is this case within the jurisdiction's statutory notice radius of any watched address? If yes, a MAPLE-pattern LLM enrichment service turns 'Map Amendment ZC 24-117, R-2 to MU-4' into plain language, carries the hearing date and the comment deadline as a first-class object, and offers a one-tap 'I oppose / support / have questions' flow that drafts verified testimony (address->district geocoding proves you're a real affected neighbor, which officials weight over form-letter floods). The novel primitive is the SPATIAL JOIN of a private address against the city's own statutory-notice radius — productizing the legal notice mechanism itself.
builds_on: MapIt (mySociety) address->jurisdiction/parcel primitive
ArcGIS REST feature services for zoning-case layers
BLDS open permit standard on Socrata
Open Civic Data Events schema
MAPLE-pattern separate LLM enrichment service + follow->event->feed->digest engine
City Scrapers Scrapy pattern + vanished-meeting-cancelled diff
curated.gap_filled: Fills Gap 2 (land use legible/actionable) and Gap 1 (comment-period deadline as alertable object) at the most granular scale. Not a duplicate of coUrbanize/PublicInput (government-buyer per-project silos) nor Symbium (permit-feasibility lookup). The spatial join against statutory-notice radius is net-new for consumers.
curated.why_now: BLDS plus the proliferation of city ArcGIS Open Data portals mean parcel-level case and permit geometry is queryable via stable REST/SODA APIs for cities covering most of the US population — proximity watches without HTML scraping. Cheap RuleBox-style classifiers make per-case plain-language translation affordable at firehose scale.
merged_from: Lot Line — an address-radius watch for development next door

curated.title: Block Whip — turn one neighbor's alert into organized, consensus-backed, verified hearing turnout
curated.one_liner: When a development or zoning case hits your block, the first neighbor who sees it can rally the exact set of addresses inside the legal notice radius into a deduplicated set of distinct verified comments, a consensus statement, and an RSVP'd hearing turnout — with a signature threshold that triggers a mandatory response.
curated.problem: A single resident commenting gets ignored and identical form-letter floods get discounted, but ten DISTINCT verified neighbors from the affected radius is decisive. Today neighbors have no way to find each other around a specific case — they rely on a lamppost flyer, a chaotic group text, or a Nextdoor thread full of non-affected people and trolls. No tool scopes organizing to exactly the legally-affected parcels and produces the quality-over-volume testimony officials weight, then converts it into a formal demand.
curated.target_user: The one civically-motivated neighbor on a block (often a homeowner or a renter facing displacement) willing to organize but lacking tools to reach exactly the right people — plus the busy neighbors who would sign on if it took two minutes and felt legitimate.
curated.solution: Built on the Lot Line watch layer: when a case is detected the system knows the exact parcel set inside the statutory notice radius (the same set the city must legally mail). An organizer 'adopts' the case and gets a campaign page scoped to those parcels. Neighbors join via address verification (geocoded to confirm they're inside the radius; SMS/postal tiers). Pol.is-style clustering surfaces CROSS-FACTIONAL consensus ('we're not anti-housing, we want the setback and traffic study fixed') as the testimony spine. Each neighbor submits a DISTINCT personalized comment (LLM helps, system prevents identical text). At a defined threshold it compiles a verified-signer testimony packet invoking any local mandatory-response requirement (Join.gov.tw mechanic) and delivers it to the clerk/commission before the hearing. The impact loop closes by ingesting the vote/disposition from Legistar/ArcGIS. Start with 2-3 clean-parcel Legistar cities to dodge the 'live national register' trap.
builds_on: Lot Line / parcel-radius watch layer
Pol.is / Computational Democracy consensus clustering
Address->district geocoding + SMS/postal verification tiers
Legistar (Granicus) OData API for agendas + vote/disposition
g0v Join.gov.tw signature->mandatory-response mechanic
WriteToThem postcode->reps send flow
City Scrapers (Scrapy) for non-Legistar agendas
curated.gap_filled: Fills Gap 5 (monitor->act->see-impact), Gap 4 (guided action), and Gap 2 (land-use organizing) with explicit anti-astroturf design. Not a duplicate of Decidim/Consul (AGPL, gov-hosted — we build thin case-scoped flows), Nextdoor (no scoping/verification/output), or Resistbot/5 Calls (Congress-only).
curated.why_now: The statutory mailed-notice radius gives a legally-defined, defensible boundary for 'who counts,' solving astroturf-discounting structurally. Pol.is is open and battle-tested; Legistar's auth-free OData exposes hearing outcomes for ~70% of large cities, making impact-loop closure real.
merged_from: Block Whip — turn one neighbor's alert into an organized hearing turnout
Neighbor Bloc: signature-to-mandatory-response organizing for local land-use cases

curated.title: Permit Pulse — neighborhood permit-and-project digest with a 'this affects you, here's the lever' nudge
curated.one_liner: A weekly plain-language digest of every new building permit, demolition, curb-cut, and land-use application in your neighborhood, ranked by how much it likely affects you, surfacing the rare items with a still-open action window.
curated.problem: Most development that reshapes a neighborhood isn't a dramatic rezoning — it's the steady drip of demolitions, by-right additions, ADUs, curb cuts, alcohol/cannabis licenses, and grading permits that individually fly under the radar but collectively reshape a block. Residents have no aggregated, legible view, and for most of it the action window (administrative protest, design-review comment, license objection) is short, obscure, and never surfaced as a deadline. The data exists in BLDS/Socrata/Accela but as raw permit codes residents can't parse.
curated.target_user: Neighborhood association leaders, local small-business owners, and engaged residents who want a low-effort 'what's changing around here' feed plus a clear signal on the rare items where their input still has a legal window.
curated.solution: Ingest BLDS permit data from Socrata across adopter cities and Accela Citizen Access where exposed, plus license/zoning feeds, normalized to OCD. A RuleBox-style LLM-generates-rules pipeline classifies each raw permit into resident-meaningful categories ('teardown/new build', 'big addition', 'new bar', 'curb cut affecting parking', 'cell tower', 'grading near slope') and scores impact by type x proximity. It tags which items still have an OPEN ACTION WINDOW and elevates those as first-class deadline objects. The weekly digest leads with 'Nothing needs you this week' or '2 items have an open window' so busy people trust it. Action paths route to the right channel or escalate into a Block Whip campaign. Cofacts-style cached community answers ('what does a Type V demolition permit mean?') get cheaper per query over time.
builds_on: BLDS open permit standard on Socrata + Accela Citizen Access
RuleBox (mySociety) LLM-generated classification rules at firehose scale
TheyWorkForYou concept-subscription + weekly-digest pattern
Open Civic Data normalization model
Cofacts community-verified cached-answer pattern
spicy-regs DuckDB+MCP pattern as model for an LLM-queryable normalized local corpus
curated.gap_filled: Fills Gap 3 (cross-source normalization of raw permit vocab) and Gap 4 (plain-language legibility), surfacing otherwise-invisible administrative windows (Gap 1). Not a duplicate of buildingeye/BuildZoom (search/lead tools, no impact scoring or action window) nor City Tracker AI/coUrbanize (project-page slivers).
curated.why_now: BLDS adoption gives a cross-city permit schema for the first time, and RuleBox-style classifiers make translating millions of cryptic permit rows into impact-ranked categories economically viable — the unglamorous normalization moat 'adjacency creep' competitors have no incentive to build.
merged_from: Permit Pulse — neighborhood permit-and-project digest with a 'this affects you, here's the lever' nudge

curated.title: ParcelWatch — open zoning/land-use change feed keyed to your address (infra)
curated.one_liner: A location-to-land-use-event service that turns the mess of Accela/Socrata/ArcGIS/Legistar into a single normalized stream of zoning changes, variances, and development hearings subscribable by parcel or radius, that other apps build on.
curated.problem: Land use, zoning, and permits for residents are entirely uncovered: the data is scattered across Accela portals, Socrata tables, ArcGIS parcel layers, and Legistar planning agendas, each in raw local vocabulary. A resident can't answer 'is anything being rezoned or built near me, and is there a hearing I can speak at?' Advocacy groups, local-news outlets, and proptech apps all rebuild fragile per-city scrapers.
curated.target_user: Primary payer: B2B2C — neighborhood associations, local-news outlets, real-estate/proptech apps, and advocacy orgs that embed or resell the feed. Beneficiary: the resident who gets an address-based alert (and the consumer Lot Line/Block Whip/Permit Pulse layers, which consume this).
curated.solution: Build the missing 'what's changing on the land near me' normalization layer, scoped city-by-city rather than promising a live national register (heeding Keep-It-In-The-Community). Per launch city: connect Legistar for planning/zoning agenda items, pull Socrata/ArcGIS permit and rezoning datasets, add City-Scrapers Scrapy spiders with the diff/'mark-vanished-as-cancelled' pattern for the Accela/PDF long tail. Normalize each event to an OCD Event with parcel geometry, a plain-language change type, hearing date, and comment endpoint. Expose a geocode->events API and radius/parcel subscriptions. RuleBox-style classifiers map raw local permit codes to a normalized taxonomy. Open-core: open the OCD-mapped schema and one reference city; charge per-city coverage and embedding. This is the shared backend the consumer land-use products run on.
builds_on: Legistar Web API (planning/zoning agendas)
City Scrapers Scrapy + diff/cancelled pipeline
Socrata + ArcGIS municipal open-data + parcel GIS
MapIt-style geocode->jurisdiction primitive
Open Civic Data Event model + OCD-IDs
mySociety RuleBox classification rules
curated.gap_filled: Fills Gap 2 (land use legible/actionable — clearest whitespace) plus Gap 3 (cross-source normalization), as reusable infrastructure. Not a duplicate of coUrbanize (developer per-project), Symbium (parcel compliance lookup), City Tracker AI (NYC analytics, no action), or Granicus EngagementHQ (per-project gov silo). None offer a cross-source, address-subscribable, normalized land-use event API.
curated.why_now: Legistar covers ~70% of large cities auth-free, Socrata/ArcGIS open-data mandates have matured so raw parcel/permit layers are increasingly public, and RuleBox makes normalizing thousands of idiosyncratic permit codes economically feasible.
merged_from: ParcelWatch — open zoning/land-use change feed keyed to your address

curated.title: RuleRadar for Small Operators — 'this rule changes how you run your business'
curated.one_liner: A vertical alert + guided-comment service that tells a specific class of small operator (independent restaurants, family farms, home daycares, owner-operator truckers) exactly which open federal/state proposed rules touch their operation and helps them write a comment grounded in their own real facts.
curated.problem: Small business owners and sole proprietors are directly regulated by FDA, USDA, DOT/FMCSA, OSHA, EPA, and state boards but never see proposed rules until they're final and compliance is mandatory. Regulations.gov has the data but zero discovery: you can't say 'I run a 12-table BYOB restaurant in Ohio' and get back the 3 open dockets that change your obligations. Trade associations cover dues-paying members; the long tail of non-member operators is flying blind while only big incumbents file comments.
curated.target_user: Owner-operators and very small business owners in heavily-regulated verticals who are NOT members of a well-staffed trade association — the independent restaurant owner, the 3-truck logistics outfit, the home childcare provider, the small specialty-crop farmer.
curated.solution: User picks a vertical profile and a few operation attributes (size, state, what they make/sell/transport). Continuously query Mirrulations/spicy-regs (DuckDB+Parquet over the full corpus) plus the Federal Register API for newly-opened dockets, and run RuleBox-style LLM-generated rules mapping dockets to vertical profiles cheaply at firehose scale. When an open-comment docket matches (is_open_for_comment + comment_end_date), the user gets a plain-language 'this affects you because…' alert with the deadline as a first-class countdown. Guided drafting asks for the operator's OWN specifics so each comment is substantively unique. Comments submit via the Regulations.gov v4 API; the operator gets the tracking number back and a notification when the final rule publishes showing whether the agency addressed comments like theirs.
builds_on: Mirrulations (full Regulations.gov S3 mirror)
spicy-regs (Parquet + DuckDB + MCP)
mirrulations-search schema (is_open_for_comment, comment dates, collections)
Federal Register API (new-docket firehose)
Regulations.gov v4 API (submission + tracking)
mySociety RuleBox (LLM-generated classification at cheap scale)
curated.gap_filled: Fills Gap 1 (comment deadlines first-class) and Gap 4 (legibility + guided action) for a horizontal small-operator audience. Not a duplicate of Quorum/FiscalNote (B2B lobbyist, no small-operator onboarding) nor Regulations.gov (no discovery/profiling/drafting). The vertical-profile->docket ruleset gets cheaper/more accurate over time (Cofacts cache economics).
curated.why_now: spicy-regs + Mirrulations make LLM SQL across the historical corpus cost cents instead of hammering the rate-limited live API, and RuleBox proves LLM-authored rules run firehose-scale on cheap infra. Cheap long-context LLMs make per-operator translation viable. 2025-2026 rule churn means more open dockets that matter.
merged_from: RuleRadar for Small Operators — "This rule changes how you run your business"

curated.title: ScopeWatch — regulatory radar for independent clinicians and small private practices
curated.one_liner: A 'lite in-house counsel' that watches the federal and state rules changing how independent clinicians get paid and what they're allowed to do, turning each into a 'does this hit me / here's the comment you can file' card.
curated.problem: Solo and small-group clinicians (NPs, PTs, optometrists, dentists, behavioral health, independent PCPs) are governed by a relentless drip of rulemaking they can't track: annual CMS Physician Fee Schedule and Quality Payment Program rules, state Medicaid bulletins, state board scope-of-practice changes, prior-authorization rules. These materially change income and legal exposure, comment periods are open and short, and only hospital systems and trade-association lobbyists with full-time staff watch. The independent finds out after the rule is final, when a claim is denied.
curated.target_user: Owner-operators of independent/small clinical practices (1-15 providers) — NPs, PTs/OTs, optometrists, dentists, mental-health clinicians, independent PCPs — and small practice managers without a compliance department.
curated.solution: Onboard by specialty + state + payer mix + ZIP. Ingest Regulations.gov v4 + Federal Register for CMS/HHS dockets, backfilled against Mirrulations/spicy-regs so an LLM can SQL relevant historical dockets cheaply. Layer Open States/Plural v3 for state scope-of-practice and Medicaid. Use RuleBox to have an LLM GENERATE classification rules once per specialty, then run them over the firehose so each user sees only rules tagged to their specialty/state/payer mix. Comment deadlines promoted to first-class alertable objects with countdown and calendar push. Plain-language card per rule: 'What it is / Does it hit your practice / Dollar-impact direction / Deadline / Draft a comment.' Guided composer drafts a substantive individualized comment, submits to Regulations.gov, archives a versioned receipt. Impact loop: when finalized, pull the response-to-comments and show whether the user's concern was addressed.
builds_on: Regulations.gov v4 + Federal Register API
Mirrulations + spicy-regs Parquet/DuckDB/MCP
Open States / Plural v3 for state scope-of-practice and Medicaid
mySociety RuleBox (LLM-generated rules at firehose scale)
MAPLE patterns (follow->feed->digest; versioned archive; separate enrichment)
Open Civic Data schema
curated.gap_filled: Primarily Gaps 1 (CMS-docket deadlines as alertable objects tied to clinician income) and 4 (legibility + guided action). Not a duplicate of Quorum/FiscalNote (priced out of solo practice, no 'does this hit MY panel') nor Regulations.gov (no discovery). The specialty+payer-mix filter is the defensible wedge against horizontal adjacency-creep.
curated.why_now: Annual CMS conversion-factor fights, expiring telehealth flexibilities, and state-by-state scope-of-practice expansion are acute in 2024-2026; RuleBox plus the spicy-regs MCP corpus make per-specialty firehose classification cheap enough for a low-ARPU audience previously uneconomical to serve.
merged_from: ScopeWatch — regulatory radar for independent clinicians and small private practices

curated.title: CodeRed — health-code, licensing, and ordinance radar for independent restaurants and food-service
curated.one_liner: A pocket compliance officer for independent restaurants and food trucks that monitors the city/county rules, fees, and ordinances that can shut them down or raise their costs — and tells them when there's still time to object.
curated.problem: Independent restaurant, cafe, food-truck, and bodega owners operate on thin margins and are blindsided by local actions: health-code revisions, license/permit fee hikes, sidewalk-dining and parklet ordinance changes, minimum-wage and scheduling ordinances, single-use-plastic and organics mandates, sign rules, and conditional-use decisions for their block. These are decided in council and board-of-health meetings with comment windows the owner never hears about, sourced in Legistar agendas and PDF packets. By the time they learn, it's law. They have no lawyer and no association rep watching their specific city.
curated.target_user: Owner-operators of independent food-service businesses (single-location restaurants, cafes, food trucks, caterers, corner stores) in mid-to-large US cities/counties, plus small local restaurant associations and chambers acting on their behalf.
curated.solution: Onboard by business address + business type. Resolve to governing jurisdictions and council district with a MapIt-style clone. Pull local legislative activity from Legistar OData (~70% of large cities/counties auth-free) — bills, agendas, committee items, board-of-health calendars. For the long tail deploy City Scrapers (Scrapy->OCD Events) with the vanished-meeting diff. Normalize raw local vocabulary ('parklet', 'sidewalk cafe permit', 'mobile food vending', 'A-frame sign') into a consumer-facing canonical set using RuleBox classifiers. Surface upcoming items and comment/hearing windows as first-class deadline objects. Each item becomes a plain-language card with a 2-minute comment composer routed to the right council member (WriteToThem pattern), plus a 'rally my block' share so nearby owners co-sign. Track items through to vote and show how the member voted and whether language changed.
builds_on: Legistar (Granicus) OData Web API
City Scrapers (Scrapy->OCD Events) + cancelled-meeting diff
MapIt clone for address->jurisdictions/council district
mySociety WriteToThem + RuleBox
MAPLE follow->event->feed->digest + versioned archive
Open Civic Data Events/People schema
curated.gap_filled: Gaps 1 (local hearing/comment deadlines), 3 (normalizing fragmented local vocab), 4 (plain-language + guided action), and the organize slice of 5. Not a duplicate of Granicus EngagementHQ/PublicInput (gov-sold, per-project silos), Quorum/FiscalNote (federal/state B2B), 5 Calls/Resistbot (Congress-only), or coUrbanize (project-specific). The address+business-type filter on Legistar is the wedge.
curated.why_now: Post-pandemic cities churn sidewalk-dining/parklet ordinances, plastics/organics mandates, and fees faster than ever, while Legistar's broad auth-free coverage plus LLM topic classification finally make affordable personalized local monitoring feasible for a fragmented low-ARPU SMB audience nobody serves.
merged_from: CodeRed — health-code, licensing, and ordinance radar for independent restaurant and food-service owners

curated.title: Watershed Watch — development-and-permit radar for local environmental and watershed groups
curated.one_liner: A monitoring + mobilization tool for small volunteer environmental groups that watches the permits, comment periods, and council/agency actions affecting their specific watershed or open space, turning each into a ready-to-mobilize action with a measurable outcome.
curated.problem: Small volunteer-run environmental groups (watershed alliances, friends-of-the-creek, land trusts) are perpetually late to the fights that matter most: a development's environmental review, a stormwater/wetlands permit, an EPA/state comment period, a rezoning that paves a floodplain, a board-of-supervisors creek vote. Signals are scattered across federal Regulations.gov dockets, the Federal Register, state legislation, and — hardest — local council agendas and Accela/Socrata/ArcGIS land-use systems. These groups have passion but no policy staff, miss short windows, submit weak duplicate comments officials discount, and burn out because they can't see whether their effort changed anything.
curated.target_user: Volunteer leaders and small staff of local environmental/watershed/conservation groups and land trusts focused on a defined geographic area (a creek, bay, forest, airshed, or set of neighborhoods).
curated.solution: Define a watch area by drawing/selecting a watershed or jurisdiction boundary; resolve to governing jurisdictions and council districts via a MapIt-style service. Monitor three streams normalized into one feed: (a) federal environmental dockets via Regulations.gov v4 + Federal Register, with Mirrulations/spicy-regs for context; (b) state environmental legislation via Open States/Plural v3; (c) local land-use/permit actions via Legistar + City Scrapers, plus best-effort Accela/Socrata/ArcGIS ingestion made legible as 'a project at [address] in your watershed is in environmental review.' Promote every comment period and hearing to a first-class deadline object. Each item is a plain-language card. The action layer differentiates on QUALITY: a Pol.is-style step surfaces cross-factional consensus, then a guided composer turns it into individualized substantive comments and routes turnout. Impact loop closes the sustainability gap: archive versioned testimony, pull response-to-comments and vote records, and show 'your 40 comments + 12 speakers -> the wetland buffer was widened.' Funded by a SocietyWorks-style B2G/foundation tier selling aggregated anonymized watershed-engagement dashboards to land trusts, foundations, and regional water boards.
builds_on: Regulations.gov v4 + Federal Register; Mirrulations + spicy-regs
Open States / Plural v3 for state environmental legislation
Legistar OData + City Scrapers for local land-use
Accela / Socrata / ArcGIS parcel-permit feeds (gap 2 frontier)
MapIt clone for boundary->jurisdictions
Pol.is / Computational Democracy for consensus
MAPLE versioned archive + notification engine; RuleBox + WriteToThem
curated.gap_filled: Hits the most gaps: 1, 2 (land-use frontier), 3 (cross-source normalization into one watershed feed), 4, and 5 (full loop with a built-in burnout/sustainability answer). Not a duplicate: geography-and-mission-scoped for amateur groups, not the lobbyist B2B incumbents; Granicus/PublicInput are gov-side; City Tracker AI/Symbium are slivers without the organize+impact loop or environmental-docket integration. Funding model is part of the idea.
curated.why_now: Land-use and environmental-review fights intensify with housing/infrastructure pressure on floodplains, more jurisdictions expose permit data via Socrata/ArcGIS/Accela, and Pol.is deliberation plus cheap LLM classification let tiny volunteer groups produce quality-over-volume consensus testimony — at a cost a foundation tier can sustain.
merged_from: Watershed Watch — development-and-permit radar for local environmental and watershed groups

curated.title: Statehouse RuleWatch — the missing layer for state agency rulemaking
curated.one_liner: A monitor + targeted-comment product for STATE agency regulations and their comment periods — the layer between Open States' bill coverage and Regulations.gov's federal-only scope, translated into 'this affects your community.'
curated.problem: State agencies (health, environmental quality, education, licensing, utilities commissions) issue the rules most directly governing daily life, with real comment periods — but there is NO federated, normalized, alertable index of them. Open States/Plural covers state legislation and hearings but explicitly NOT regulations. Each state publishes rulemaking in a different register (Texas Register, California OAL, NY State Register) with its own vocabulary, formats, and deadline conventions. A resident has no way to learn their health department opened comment on a rule affecting, say, well-water testing or assisted-living staffing.
curated.target_user: Residents and small community/advocacy groups who care about a state-level issue (water quality, school standards, eldercare, utility rates, occupational licensing) but can't read 50 administrative registers — and local advocates who have no tool for state rulemaking the way they have Open States for bills.
curated.solution: Build a normalization pipeline (City Scrapers/Scrapy, one scraper per register since there's no API) that ingests state administrative registers and proposed-rule notices into the OCD model with OCD-IDs, extracting the comment deadline as a first-class object. RuleBox-style rules normalize each state's raw vocabulary into shared topic concepts so a non-expert can subscribe to 'drinking water' or 'nursing home staffing' nationally and get matches regardless of local terminology (TheyWorkForYou concept-subscribe + term-expansion). MapIt-style geocoding ties a rule's geographic scope to the user. Guided drafting + submission routes to each state's actual channel (web form, email, or generated cover letter for PDF-only states — realistic, MAPLE-style). Subscribers get a weekly digest plus deadline alerts.
builds_on: Open States / Plural v3 (explicit complement; reuse people.geo and OCD-IDs)
Open Civic Data standard + OCD-IDs
City Scrapers (Scrapy) per-source scraper + diff/cancellation pipeline
MapIt (geographic scope->resident)
mySociety RuleBox (normalization across heterogeneous state vocab)
TheyWorkForYou concept-subscription + term-expansion + weekly digest
curated.gap_filled: Fills Gap 1 (deadlines first-class), Gap 3 (cross-jurisdiction normalization of 50 registers), and Gap 4. Not a duplicate: Open States/Plural deliberately excludes rulemaking; Regulations.gov is federal-only; Quorum/FiscalNote treat state regs as B2B compliance, not resident-facing 'affects your community' alerts. The scraper-built normalized state-rulemaking corpus is the moat.
curated.why_now: More policy pushes to the states in 2025-2026, raising the stakes of state rulemaking precisely as it remains the least legible layer. LLM-generated scrapers + RuleBox normalization make unifying 50 idiosyncratic registers newly feasible, and Open States has proven the consumption-over-self-hosting model and OCD schema this layer plugs into.
merged_from: Statehouse RuleWatch — the missing layer for state agency rulemaking

curated.title: Consensus Comment — turn scattered residents into one statement officials can't ignore
curated.one_liner: A Pol.is-style deliberation room attached to any live comment period (federal rule, state rulemaking, local hearing) that converts scattered opinions into ranked cross-factional consensus statements and files the result as a single weighted, deduplicated packet on the official record.
curated.problem: When a rule or zoning change drops, residents either do nothing or flood the docket with identical form letters officials are trained to discount as astroturf. The genuinely persuasive thing — 'here is what 80% of us across the political spectrum actually agree on' — never gets produced because nobody runs the deliberation, and no tool both clusters opinion AND files the result officially. Felt acutely by the informal organizer who can rally bodies but can't synthesize them, and who quits because the docket-level effort is invisible.
curated.target_user: The volunteer neighborhood organizer / HOA-adjacent resident who can get 50-300 neighbors mildly engaged on a specific rule, zoning case, or federal comment period but lacks tooling to make that engagement count at docket scale.
curated.solution: For any item with an open comment window (Regulations.gov commentEndDate, Federal Register doc, Legistar agenda item), spin up a lightweight Pol.is-derived room. An LLM pre-summarizes the docket (via Mirrulations/spicy-regs) into plain-language statement seeds to bootstrap it. Participants vote agree/disagree/pass and add statements; clustering surfaces statements winning majorities ACROSS opinion clusters (the cross-factional consensus vTaiwan proved officials respect). At deadline the tool generates a structured packet — consensus statements ranked by cross-cluster support, N-of-M signer counts, address-geocoded district verification — and submits via Regulations.gov v4 (federal) or as routable testimony for Legistar municipalities. Each signer can also file a short individual comment so volume AND quality land. Funding: SocietyWorks-style B2G — sell agencies and clerks a 'consensus dashboard' so the quality signal is legible to them too.
builds_on: Pol.is / Computational Democracy clustering
Regulations.gov v4 API (commentEndDate + submission)
Federal Register API
Legistar OData API for municipal hearing items
MAPLE testimony-routing + versioned archive
MapIt-style address->district geocoding for signer verification
Open Civic Data IDs to key each room to a canonical item
curated.gap_filled: Fills Gap 1 (deadline as trigger), Gap 4 (guided action), and Gap 5 (loop) — and is the docket-scale/cross-jurisdiction complement to Block Whip's parcel-radius scope. Not a duplicate: Pol.is/vTaiwan has no comment-period ingestion or official-submission pipe; Resistbot/5 Calls do volume with no clustering or discovery; Granicus EngagementHQ is a per-project gov silo.
curated.why_now: Mirrulations + spicy-regs make the full corpus cheaply queryable so an LLM can pre-summarize any docket into statement seeds; Pol.is clustering is open and proven; officials' fatigue with form-letter floods makes a credible quality signal more valuable than ever.
merged_from: Consensus Comment: turn 200 angry neighbors into one statement officials can't ignore

curated.title: Comment Workshop — verified, substantive comments engineered to survive the form-letter filter, built into a weekly habit
curated.one_liner: A guided drafting tool that turns a person's lived experience into a unique, verified, on-the-record comment deliberately built to beat the 'discount the form-letter flood' filter — wrapped in a light weekly civic habit so the muscle actually forms.
curated.problem: Two linked failures. curated.problem: No system anywhere exposes 'comment-period deadline' as a queryable, alertable object. curated.target_user: Primary payer: city/county clerks, planning departments, and small federal/state agency offices that run comment periods and want verified, structured, de-duplicated input — plus developers and civic orgs building action tools on the API.
